factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D does NOT support Capture One RAW or tethering; Phocus is Hasselblad's native tethering solution
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly says the Hasselblad X2D 100C is “Not supported” in Capture One and that tethering is only via Hasselblad’s Phocus, identifying Phocus as Hasselblad’s native tethering solution.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Fujifilm GFX100 II X-sync or flash sync speed as 1/125s or 1/125 second
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states the Fujifilm GFX100 II max sync speed as 1/125s in the “Studio Strobe Sync Reliability & Strobe Integration” section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D XCD leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/2000s or 1/4000s with specific lens models
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Hasselblad X2D native XCD leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/2000s for most lenses and up to 1/4000s for newer V/E series lenses, explicitly tying the higher sync speed to specific lens series/models.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One XF IQ4 with Blue Ring/Schneider-Kreuznach leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/1600s
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the Phase One XF IQ4 150MP with Schneider Kreuznach LS (leaf-shutter) lenses provides flash sync up to 1/1600s, noting it is lens dependent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States both Fujifilm GFX100 II and Phase One IQ4 support Capture One for tethering or RAW processing
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly says the Fujifilm GFX100 II is “fully supported” in Capture One and that the Phase One XF IQ4 150MP has the deepest Capture One integration, indicating both support Capture One for tethering/RAW processing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One IQ4 supports Ethernet, Bonjour, or network tethering capability to Capture One
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the Phase One XF IQ4 150MP supports “PoE Ethernet tethering” and describes it as part of its deep Capture One integration, which implies network tethering capability to Capture One.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Fujifilm GFX100 II body price between $7,000-8,500
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Fujifilm GFX100 II body price as “$7,800–8,500,” which falls within the required $7,000–8,500 range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D body price between $7,000-8,500
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists the Hasselblad X2D 100C body price as “$8,000–8,600,” which falls within the required $7,000–8,500 range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One XF IQ4 complete system between $50,000-70,000 for body, back, and lenses
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives a Phase One XF IQ4 150MP “Body/Back” price range of $49,000–56,000 and a full kit total of $58,000–$72,000, but it does not explicitly state that a complete system (body, back, and lenses) is between $50,000–70,000. The required $50,000–70,000 complete-system range is not directly provided.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Compares 3-prime lens kit costs (Fuji $6-8k range, Hasselblad $10-12k range, Phase One $15-20k range)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response provides three-prime kit cost ranges for each brand, listing Fuji three primes at $4,800–6,000, Hasselblad at $9,900–12,900, and Phase One at $15,000–19,000, which align with the requested $6–8k, $10–12k, and $15–20k comparison ranges.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Names at least 2 rental providers for medium-format backup bodies (e.g., FotoCare, Adorama Rental, Lensrentals)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response names multiple NYC rental providers for backup bodies, including FotoCare, Capture Integration, Root NYC, and FutureCapture, satisfying the requirement to list at least two.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D has 1TB internal SSD storage
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states under the Hasselblad X2D 100C workflow speed section that it has “internal 1TB SSD (fast),” which satisfies the requirement.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies Hasselblad color science as HNCS or Hasselblad Natural Colour Solution
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly identifies Hasselblad color science as “HNCS Color Science” in the Color Science & Skin Tone Rendering section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States or implies Hasselblad X2D works with Capture One for tethering or RAW processing
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly says the Hasselblad X2D 100C is “Not supported” in Capture One and that tethering is only via Phocus, so it does not state or imply X2D works with Capture One for tethering or RAW processing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides conditional recommendations mapping each system to specific user priorities or use case scenarios
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response ends with explicit recommendations that tie each system to different priorities and scenarios (e.g., Fuji for pragmatic high-volume NYC fashion work, Hasselblad for skin tones/leaf-shutter strobe creativity with Phocus tolerance, Phase One for ultra-high-end color-critical shoots with digital tech workflows). These are conditional mappings based on workflow needs, pace, and budget tolerance.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies Hasselblad requires Phocus (not Capture One) as a strategic workflow ecosystem constraint
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly states that the Hasselblad X2D 100C is not supported by Capture One and that tethering is only possible via Hasselblad’s own Phocus, framing this as a significant workflow limitation/constraint.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes Phase One network/Ethernet tethering from USB-only tethering as workflow architecture advantage
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly highlights Phase One’s “PoE Ethernet tethering (rock-solid, long-distance)” and contrasts it with Fujifilm’s “wired USB-C tethering,” framing Ethernet/network tethering as a reliability/workflow advantage beyond USB-only tethering.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains HSS mode involves power loss vs leaf-shutter sync maintaining full flash power at high shutter speeds
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly contrasts HSS on the Fuji as having significant power loss with leaf-shutter sync on Hasselblad/Phase One allowing full-power flash at high shutter speeds (e.g., up to 1/2000–1/4000s). This directly explains the power-loss vs full-power distinction requested.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Projects total ownership costs over multiple years including depreciation, subscriptions, or recurring fees
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes a dedicated “Depreciation & TCO (3-year)” section with estimated 3-year total cost of ownership ranges per system, and it also accounts for recurring software costs via a “Software Subscriptions” subsection (Capture One Pro yearly pricing and Phocus being free).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides NYC-specific rental coverage or support availability information for the mentioned New York location
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes NYC-specific backup rental support information by naming New York rental houses (e.g., FotoCare, Capture Integration, Root NYC, FutureCapture) and describing relative stock availability and swap/repair considerations for Fuji/Phase vs. Hasselblad in NYC.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly corrects at least one common misconception about these systems with supporting documentation
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly corrects a misconception by stating that the Hasselblad X2D 100C is not supported in Capture One and cites supporting documentation (Capture One supported camera list and a Capture One feature request link) to substantiate the point.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: States professional fashion photography doesn't need the specified features or dismisses the user's technical requirements as unnecessary
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not dismiss the user’s requirements as unnecessary or claim professional fashion photography doesn’t need the specified features. Instead, it treats the criteria (sync reliability, Capture One tethering, color accuracy, workflow speed, costs, rentals) as important and evaluates the cameras against them.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses domain-specific photography terminology (leaf shutter, focal-plane shutter, tethering, X-sync, HSS, CFexpress, PDAF)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple domain-specific photography terms, including “leaf shutter,” “focal-plane shutter,” “High-Speed Sync (HSS),” “CFexpress,” “tethering,” and “sync”/“XCD lenses are leaf-shutter.”

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes information by comparison criteria with headings, bullets, or tables for each evaluation dimension
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized into numbered sections with clear headings for each evaluation dimension (sync reliability, tethering/Capture One, color science, workflow speed, lens ecosystem, costs, TCO/rentals) and uses bullet lists and tables to compare the three systems within each section.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional advisory tone without marketing language, brand bias, or unsupported superlatives
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses marketing-style and biased superlatives such as “gold standard,” “universally acclaimed,” “unmatched,” “industry gold standard,” and “reference tool,” and makes broad claims like “every section directly references…authoritative sources” without substantiating them in-text.

presentation-quality.requirement: Explains fundamental photography concepts like ISO, aperture, or RAW files that a Canon R5 professional would already know
presentation-quality.explanation: The response does not explain basic photography fundamentals like ISO, aperture, or what RAW files are. It stays focused on medium-format system comparisons (sync, tethering, color, workflow, costs) without introductory-level definitions a Canon R5 professional would already know.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents camera system comparisons as narrative prose paragraphs instead of structured tables, lists, or side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is primarily written as narrative prose with multiple paragraph-style sections (e.g., sync reliability, tethering, color science, workflow speed, costs, and conclusion). Although it includes one lens table, the overall comparisons are not presented predominantly in structured tables/lists/side-by-side format.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses casual language patterns like "honestly", "I think", "seems like a pain", or hedging phrases that undermine professional authority
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses hedging/casual phrasing such as “often selected,” “many pros cite,” “described as,” and “considered industry gold standard,” which softens authority and relies on subjective testimonials rather than firm statements in several sections (e.g., color science and tethering summaries).

citation-quality.requirement: Cites official manufacturer sites (fujifilm-x.com, hasselblad.com, phaseone.com) for camera specifications
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites official manufacturer sources for specifications, including Fujifilm’s official site (fujifilm-x.com) and Phase One’s official site (phaseone.com). It does not cite Hasselblad’s official site (hasselblad.com), only linking to a Hasselblad page on Phocus and an X2D FAQ.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Capture One support pages (support.captureone.com) for camera compatibility and tethering capabilities
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites Capture One support pages on support.captureone.com, including the “Camera Models and RAW Files Supported by Capture One” article, as evidence for camera compatibility and tethering-related support.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Schneider-Kreuznach documentation (schneiderkreuznach.com) for Phase One Blue Ring leaf-shutter lens sync speeds
citation-quality.explanation: The response discusses Phase One Schneider Kreuznach LS lenses and cites sources like Capture Integration and a Phase One PDF, but it does not cite any Schneider-Kreuznach documentation from schneiderkreuznach.com regarding Blue Ring leaf-shutter lens sync speeds.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites authorized dealers or manufacturer stores (B&H Photo, manufacturer direct stores, Digital Transitions) for pricing
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites authorized/pro dealers and manufacturer sources for pricing, including FotoCare (authorized dealer), Fujifilm and Phase One official product pages, and Capture Integration/Digital Transitions (digitalback.com) for Phase One pricing.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites established rental providers (FotoCare, Adorama Rental, Lensrentals, or similar) for backup body availability
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites established rental providers for backup availability, explicitly naming FotoCare and also listing other NYC rental houses (e.g., Capture Integration, Root NYC, FutureCapture) in the “Rental House Backup” section and sources.

citation-quality.requirement: Bases specific claims on "I heard" or secondhand information instead of citing documented sources
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not base claims on “I heard” or other secondhand phrasing. It presents assertions as factual and includes a sources section with many links rather than relying on hearsay.
